source_paper_title: Robust Online Selection with Uncertain Offer Acceptance
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Perez_Salazar_2025, title={Robust Online Selection with Uncertain Offer Acceptance}, volume={50}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0210}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0210}, number={3}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Perez-Salazar, Sebastian and Singh, Mohit and Toriello, Alejandro}, year={2025}, month=aug, pages={2226–2260} }
context:
Section 9 (Concluding Remarks), page 24, paragraph beginning “We empirically observe …”

We empirically observe that the robust ratio \(\gamma_n^*(p)\) is convex and decreasing as a function of \(p\), and thus we expect the same behavior from \(\gamma_\infty(p)\), though this remains to be proved (see Figure 1).
title: Prove convexity and monotonicity of the optimal robust ratio in acceptance probability
background_latex:
Consider the secretary problem with uncertain acceptance (SP-UA) with a known horizon length \(n\in\mathbb{N}\) and acceptance probability \(p\in(0,1]\). There are \(n\) candidates with distinct absolute ranks \(1\prec 2\prec\cdots\prec n\) (rank \(1\) is best). Candidates arrive in a uniformly random permutation \(\pi=(R_1,\dots,R_n)\) of \([n]=\{1,\dots,n\}\), where \(R_t\) is the absolute rank of the \(t\)-th arrival.

At time \(t\), the decision maker observes only the partial rank \(r_t\in[t]\) of the current candidate among the first \(t\) arrivals. The decision maker chooses either \(\text{offer}\) or \(\text{pass}\). If \(\text{offer}\) is chosen at time \(t\), the current candidate accepts independently with probability \(p\), in which case the process stops and that candidate is selected; if the offer is rejected (probability \(1-p\)), the process continues to \(t+1\). If \(\text{pass}\) is chosen, the process continues to \(t+1\). A policy \(P\) is any (possibly randomized) Markovian rule mapping \((t,r_t)\) to a distribution over \(\{\text{offer},\text{pass}\}\).

For \(k\in[n]\), a \"top \(k\)\" candidate means absolute rank in \([k]\). Define the robust ratio of a policy \(P\) at acceptance probability \(p\) as
\[
\gamma_P(p)=\min_{k\in[n]}\frac{\Pr(\text{policy }P\text{ selects a top-}k\text{ candidate and the offer is accepted})}{\Pr(\text{at least one of the top-}k\text{ candidates would accept an offer})}.
\]
Since each candidate would accept an offer with probability \(p\) independently of everything else, \(\Pr(\text{at least one top-}k\text{ candidate accepts})=1-(1-p)^k\). The optimal robust ratio is
\[
\gamma_n^*(p)=\sup_P\gamma_P(p),
\]
and the asymptotic optimal robust ratio is \(\gamma_\infty^*(p)=\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_n^*(p)\), which exists because \(\gamma_n^*(p)\) is nonincreasing in \(n\) for fixed \(p\).
problem_definition_latex: Establish (or refute) that the optimal robust ratio \(\gamma_n^*(p)\) is a convex and decreasing function of \(p\in(0,1]\) (for fixed \(n\)), and correspondingly that \(\gamma_\infty^*(p)\) is convex and decreasing in \(p\in(0,1]\).
latex:
Consider the secretary problem with uncertain acceptance (SP-UA) with a known horizon length \(n\in\mathbb{N}\) and acceptance probability \(p\in(0,1]\). There are \(n\) candidates with distinct absolute ranks \(1\prec 2\prec\cdots\prec n\) (rank \(1\) is best). Candidates arrive in a uniformly random permutation \(\pi=(R_1,\dots,R_n)\) of \([n]=\{1,\dots,n\}\), where \(R_t\) is the absolute rank of the \(t\)-th arrival.

At time \(t\), the decision maker observes only the partial rank \(r_t\in[t]\) of the current candidate among the first \(t\) arrivals. The decision maker chooses either \(\text{offer}\) or \(\text{pass}\). If \(\text{offer}\) is chosen at time \(t\), the current candidate accepts independently with probability \(p\), in which case the process stops and that candidate is selected; if the offer is rejected (probability \(1-p\)), the process continues to \(t+1\). If \(\text{pass}\) is chosen, the process continues to \(t+1\). A policy \(P\) is any (possibly randomized) Markovian rule mapping \((t,r_t)\) to a distribution over \(\{\text{offer},\text{pass}\}\).

For \(k\in[n]\), a \"top \(k\)\" candidate means absolute rank in \([k]\). Define the robust ratio of a policy \(P\) at acceptance probability \(p\) as
\[
\gamma_P(p)=\min_{k\in[n]}\frac{\Pr(\text{policy }P\text{ selects a top-}k\text{ candidate and the offer is accepted})}{\Pr(\text{at least one of the top-}k\text{ candidates would accept an offer})}.
\]
Since each candidate would accept an offer with probability \(p\) independently of everything else, \(\Pr(\text{at least one top-}k\text{ candidate accepts})=1-(1-p)^k\). The optimal robust ratio is
\[
\gamma_n^*(p)=\sup_P\gamma_P(p),
\]
and the asymptotic optimal robust ratio is \(\gamma_\infty^*(p)=\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_n^*(p)\), which exists because \(\gamma_n^*(p)\) is nonincreasing in \(n\) for fixed \(p\).

Establish (or refute) that the optimal robust ratio \(\gamma_n^*(p)\) is a convex and decreasing function of \(p\in(0,1]\) (for fixed \(n\)), and correspondingly that \(\gamma_\infty^*(p)\) is convex and decreasing in \(p\in(0,1]\).
location_in_paper: Section 9 (Concluding Remarks), page 24, paragraph beginning “We empirically observe …”
open_problem_quote: We empirically observe that the robust ratio \(\gamma_n^*(p)\) is convex and decreasing as a function of \(p\), and thus we expect the same behavior from \(\gamma_\infty(p)\), though this remains to be proved (see Figure 1).
counterexample_suitability_rationale: For fixed finite \(n\), \(\gamma_n^*(p)\) can be computed numerically by solving the paper’s LP for a grid of \(p\) values, so violations of convexity/monotonicity could be searched for computationally. However, proving (or disproving) the property for all \(p\) and all \(n\) (or in the \(n\to\infty\) limit) is not reducible to a finite instance search, and numerical evidence may miss very small or asymptotic violations.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: An evolutionary loop could parameterize policies (e.g., threshold families) and numerically approximate \(\gamma_n^*(p)\) via the LP or simulation to probe the shape in \(p\), potentially discovering candidate extremal behaviors or counterexamples for small \(n\). But establishing a general convexity/monotonicity theorem likely requires analytic arguments beyond code-driven search, and numerical fitness signals only approximate the universal claims.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The open problem from Perez-Salazar, Singh, and Toriello asks for structural properties of the optimal robust ratio \(\gamma_n^*(p)\) (and its limit \(\gamma_\infty^*(p)\)) as a function of the homogeneous acceptance probability \(p\): specifically, whether it is decreasing and convex on \((0,1]\). In the source paper, \(\gamma_n^*(p)\) is characterized exactly by a linear program derived from an MDP polyhedral description of all Markovian policies, and \(\gamma_\infty^*(p)\) is approximated by a continuous LP. The paper proves several sharp bounds and identifies an exact optimal policy for \(p\ge p^*\approx 0.594\), where \(\gamma_\infty^*(p)=p^{p/(1-p)}\), but it only reports empirical evidence (via solving \((LP)_{n,p}\) numerically) that \(\gamma_n^*(p)\) appears convex and decreasing; no proof is given.

Among the forward citations provided, only “Prophets Inequalities with Uncertain Acceptance” is available and it is not directly about the robust-ratio secretary objective. It develops structural reductions for prophet inequalities with uncertain acceptance by absorbing acceptance into realized values \(Z_i=X_iA_i\), and it discusses that some uncertain-acceptance performance measures can exhibit non-monotone behavior as acceptance probabilities vary. While this does not resolve convexity/monotonicity of \(\gamma_n^*(p)\), it suggests that proving monotonicity may require exploiting the specific normalization by \(1-(1-p)^k\) and the LP structure in the secretary setting, rather than relying on generic “more acceptance helps” intuition.

Given the current citation set, there is no known solving paper establishing convexity and monotonicity of \(\gamma_n^*(p)\) or \(\gamma_\infty^*(p)\). The problem therefore remains open. Promising directions include sensitivity/parametric analysis of \((LP)_{n,p}\) and \((CLP)_p\) with respect to \(p\), potentially via dual formulations (where \(p\) enters both feasibility and objective normalization) and envelope theorems for piecewise-linear concave value functions; another direction is to attempt to prove monotonicity/convexity first for the continuous relaxation \(\gamma_\infty^*(p)\) and then transfer to finite \(n\) using the quantitative approximation bound \(|\gamma_n^* - \gamma_\infty^*|\le O((\log n)^2/(p\sqrt n))\) from the source paper.

sources.title: Prophets Inequalities with Uncertain Acceptance
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper studies prophet inequalities with uncertain acceptance (PI-UA), where each option has value \(X_i\) and acceptance indicator \(A_i\). Key structural result (Theorem 3.1): for \(Z_i:=X_iA_i\), the optimal online value in PI-UA equals the optimal online value in the classical prophet inequality instance \((Z_1,\dots,Z_n)\), i.e. \(DP(Y_1,\dots,Y_n)=ON(Z_1,\dots,Z_n)\). It also proves a bound for scaled Bernoulli prophet instances (Theorem 4.2): if \(X_i=\lambda_i\mathrm{Ber}(p_i)\) with fixed \(\lambda_i\ge 0\), \(p_i\in(0,1)\), then \(ON(\mathcal I)/OPT(\mathcal I)\ge \tfrac12-\min_i p_i\), tight and achievable by a single-threshold algorithm.
sources.connection: The open problem concerns the secretary problem with uncertain acceptance (SP-UA) and asks whether the optimal robust ratio \(\gamma_n^*(p)\) (a worst-case-over-\(k\) probability ratio normalized by \(1-(1-p)^k\)) is convex and decreasing in the homogeneous acceptance probability \(p\). This paper instead analyzes expected-value competitive ratios in a prophet-inequality model with uncertain acceptance, with generally heterogeneous \(p_i\) and distributional values. It does not define or study \(\gamma_n^*(p)\), nor does it prove convexity/monotonicity in \(p\) for the secretary robust-ratio objective. The only tangential link is conceptual: it highlights that some performance measures in uncertain-acceptance online selection can exhibit non-monotonic behavior in acceptance likelihood (Section 6.2), which suggests monotonicity/convexity questions can be subtle, but it provides no direct result for \(\gamma_n^*(p)\).
